input: Two connected health modules: a blood pressure reading card that updates automatically with each new measurement — a simple read-only display of the latest data point. A personal blood pressure tracker that stores each reading over time and shows trends as a line chart. The reading card feeds into the tracker. Both are purely private health data.

Two connected farm modules: a product card for a single item showing item name, price per pound, weight available, and farm origin — a simple read-only display. A product catalog for that same farmer — an ordered list of all their items that visitors can sort by price and filter by category. Visitors can browse with the farmer's consent.

Two connected language learning modules: a single vocabulary flashcard showing a word, its translation, and an example sentence — a simple display card. A personal study deck — an ordered collection of flashcards for a Spanish course, sorted by difficulty level. Private study tool, no sharing.

Two connected entertainment modules: a single movie record showing title, director, genre, year, and my watch status (want to watch, watching, watched). A personal watchlist — an ordered queue of these movie records sorted by priority. Filter by genre or watch status, track my progress. Completely private.

Two connected health modules: a daily workout log — a form where I record exercises, sets, reps, and weight. Charts show strength progress over time. Completely private. A fitness dashboard that groups my workout log, step count, and sleep quality into one combined view — charts for each metric, filtered by type. I might share specific metrics with my doctor, so it asks for consent before sharing.

Two connected art modules: my personal art portfolio — a curated collection of digital artworks, sortable by medium or date. Visitors need my consent to browse it. A community art gallery that shows work from many artists in a ranked feed — visitors upvote their favorites and share pieces externally. The gallery is open to anyone without sign-up.

Two connected finance modules: my personal expense tracker — expenses organized by category (dining, transport, bills) with trend charts and filters. Everything private on my device. A shared household budget board that combines each roommate's spending into a grouped view by category and person. All roommates join by invitation before they can view or add entries.

Two connected home modules: my personal recipe book — recipes grouped by cuisine type (Italian, Mexican, Thai), filterable by ingredient, sortable by prep time. Private. A kitchen management hub combining my recipe book, grocery list, and meal planner — I navigate between recipe browsing, grocery shopping, and weekly meal planning views. All private, no sharing.

Two modules for a freelancer: a personal calendar with day, week, and month views that tracks deadlines and appointments, filters by type (work, personal). Completely private. A project coordination workspace that spatially arranges the calendar alongside a task board and an invoice log as separate working areas. The coordination space requires consent to share with clients.

Two health modules for a holistic practice: a personal health overview combining step count, sleep quality, and heart rate data — grouped view with weekly trend charts and filtering by metric type. Sharing requires consent. A personal wellness workspace that spatially arranges the health overview alongside a mood journal and meditation log as side-by-side working areas. Sharing requires the same consent as the health overview.

Two education modules: a personal reading journal — a form-based tool for writing daily reactions and reflections as I read assigned texts, with progress tracking. Just for me, completely private. A study group discussion thread where students post reflections and reply to each other — voting surfaces the best insights. New students need an invitation. My private journal entries inform my public posts in the forum.

Two science modules for a field naturalist: a personal birdwatching field log — an ordered list of sightings by date, each with species, location, and photo. Filter by species, track sightings over time. Private field notes. A field research dashboard combining the birdwatching log, local weather data, and observation notes into a grouped collection with charts. The research dashboard can share findings with a collaborating team with consent.
